Stuttgart have won four of their five home Europa League ties this season and boast one of the best home records in the Bundesliga, in which they sit in fourth.

They will make it an uncomfortable night for Porto, who have flown into Germany after back-to-back trips to Sporting and Benfica (winning neither) and they could be a little weary as a result.

Make Stuttgart the platform of an acca alongside a pair of Double Chance lines from the Conference League.

First, a modest AZ side are being over-rated at home to Czech outfit Sparta Prague, who qualified for the last 16 in fourth and have to fancy their chances.

And their Czech Liga stablemates, Sigma Olomouc, who boast the best home defence by a country mile in their league, should have no doubts they can stifle Bundesliga relegation candidates Mainz.

News.title: UK households with slow cookers face £23.09 charges from April
News.content:
UK households with slow cookers face £23.09 charges from April

Millions of Brits use slow cookers to prepare roast beef and pulled pork, and with Ofgem lowering the energy price cap from April 1, we explain how much they will cost to run

View 2 Images Slow cookers take a considerable amount of time to make meals - but are a lot cheaper (Image: Getty)

Households using their slow cooker at least once weekly will face annual costs of £23.09 from April 1. Ofgem is reducing the energy price cap from that date, resulting in lower yearly energy bills for British consumers.

‌



The energy regulator has announced that households on a standard variable tariff (default tariff) paying for electricity via Direct Debit will now be charged an average of 24.67 pence per kilowatt hour (kWh).

‌



This represents welcome news for households across Britain, who may notice savings when running particular appliances. Slow cookers have surged in popularity throughout the UK in recent years, offering significant time-saving benefits in meal preparation.

‌



Stew will taste even more delicious if 1 ingredient is added to broth READ MORE :

View 2 Images Slow cookers have become a big part of many households (Image: sumnersgraphicsinc via Getty Images)

Dishes such as roast beef and pulled pork are remarkably simple to prepare in these devices, and users can set them going before heading out to work, returning home to a fully cooked meal in the evening. But what's the true running cost of these gadgets, and would you be better off sticking with a conventional oven?

We've crunched the numbers to help you work out your expected expenditure. Our calculations are based on eight hours of operation, which represents the typical cooking time for dishes like roast beef and pork.

‌



The yearly figure we've calculated assumes weekly slow cooker usage, totalling 52 occasions annually. According to GoCompare, a typical slow cooker operates on between 200W and 250W.

Using Ofgem's revised rate of 24.67 pence per kilowatt hour (kWh), we've determined that slow cookers will, on average, cost merely £23.09 annually to operate following April 1. Here's a more in-depth breakdown, using a formula supplied by energy gurus Bluetti:.

The wattage is multiplied by the hours used and then divided by 1,000. This gives us 1.8KWh. Multiply this by the new average hourly cost, and you get 44.406p.

‌



If you're utilising your slow cooker for this duration once a week throughout the year, you'd then multiply this single-use cost by 52. This results in an annual expense of £23.09.

Whilst slow cookers may take longer to prepare your meals than an oven, they consume significantly less energy. GoCompare states that an average electric oven uses between 2,000 and 5,000 watts of energy, which is over ten times the energy consumption of a slow cooker.

According to Ofgem, the new daily standing charge is 57.21 pence per day. This figure is based on the average across England, Scotland and Wales and includes VAT at 5%.

Article continues below

This price reduction follows the Government's recent announcement that funding for two environmental and social schemes will either cease or be funded through general taxes from April 2026. As a result, energy customers will now save an average of £150, says the energy regulator.

News.title: Magaluf 'wolf pack' rape hero describes 'awful' scene after Brit teen attack
News.content:
Magaluf 'wolf pack' rape hero describes 'awful' scene after Brit teen attack

WARNING: DISTRESSING CONTENT. After enduring a horrifying gang rape in a Magaluf hotel room by a 'wolf pack' of predators, an 18-year-old girl managed to escape and get help

View 4 Images The eight men now face serious prison sentences (Image: SOLARPIX.COM/J.Bastida)

In the early hours of August 14, 2023, a hotel security guard at Magaluf's BH Mallorca Resort walked in on a scene of complete devastation.

An 18-year-old girl had been subjected to a malicious gang rape by a "wolf pack" of seven French men and one Swiss national, all of whom were intent on "humiliating and violating" the British teenager. The vile perpetrators, who struck a plea bargain deal with prosecutors mere days before their rape trial began, have since accepted prison sentences totalling just over 73 years, after being warned they could be jailed for more than 150 years if convicted.

Sickening details of the depraved 30-minute assault unfolded over the course of the investigation, during which all eight men, aged between 18 and 26 at the time of their arrests, refused to cooperate with police, attempting to maintain their silence throughout interviews with officers, and later on, the courts.

One source previously told the Mirror: "Some have never agreed to answer questions or give statements." However, disturbing videos taken by the group of eight spoke a thousand words, shedding light on the unnamed survivor's harrowing ordeal, which occurred just 400 yards away from Magaluf's famed party strip.

View 4 Images The vile attack occurred close to Magaluf's famous party strip (Image: SOLARPIX.COM)

It was at around 5am that a hotel security guard, clocking on for his early shift, discovered the teenager sobbing on the lobby floor and went to her rescue. He told The Sun: "I came in for the morning shift and found her downstairs, she was so upset. It was awful." As told to the publication, the police arrived swiftly at the hotel after the guard alerted them. He continued: 'They dealt with it very well. The police came within five minutes and quickly helped her."

It's understood that the girl received immediate medical attention and counselling, with a medic who treated her noting grip marks on her arms. It was then that a nightmarish picture of what she had endured began to emerge. In phone footage, which would provide crucial evidence in the arrests and subsequent prosecutions, the young woman could be seen being raped in an attack notable for its shocking brutality.

French tourists Khalil Abderrahmen Mejbri and Romain Charles Michel Galatioto "orally penetrated" the woman, who "fell into a state of unconsciousness" during the gang attack. Galatioto did so seven times while the teenager was naked aside from her bra. This is according to court documents signed by all men as part of their plea bargain deals.

View 4 Images The teenager was discovered sobbing in the hotel lobby by a horrified security guard (Image: SOLARPIX.COM)

French national Anthony Guy Gerard Wengler and Swiss national Haiaiel Ngandjeu Sjamen Tchatchou raped the survivor "at least once", as did Thomas Eric Coretin Wisniewski, also from France. Tchatchou and Wengler also spat at the victim and whipped her along with Mejbri after Frenchman Satilmis Sahan slapped her.

Meanwhile, French national Anthony Auque, who had been holidaying with Wengler, and fellow Frenchman Mouhamadou Wade, watched on while the 30-minute attack occurred in room 108 of the Magaluf hotel, surrounding the victim along with the rest of the men, to whom they chanted sick words of encouragement. Obscene threats and grotesque comments were shouted out, including insults such as, "There's four of us and there's a cow". Group members also "laughed out loudly" as the assault continued.

A plea agreement, signed by all eight men, reads: "Each one of the defendants with their mobile phones, during the incidents previously described, recorded several videos in which they honed in on their victim's private parts and in which they appeared obliging her to carry out sexual acts or performing acts of an identical nature upon her as aforementioned." It continues: "Boasting about the actions taken and with the clear intention of humiliating and violating his victim, Mejbri and Wade sent and shared the content of their recordings through the Snapchat app. Those recordings were taken during the time in which their victim was semi-conscious or unconscious, so that she was unable to consent to it."

View 4 Images Members of the rape gang 'laughed out loudly' during the vicious assault (Image: SOLARPIX.COM/J.Bastida)

Reports at the time the charges were made stated that three of the men had met the teenager hours earlier while out in the Spanish party town. After sexually attacking the woman at dawn, one of the perpetrators went out into a hotel corridor to encourage strangers returning from their nights out to have 'free sex' with her. Earlier this week, an insider said, "It was almost a case of them opening the door and inviting others passing by in the corridor to join them."

Mejbri and Wisniewski have been given a nine-year prison sentence for a crime of "sexual assault with carnal access", a severe form of sexual crime. Wengler, Tchatchou and Galatioto were handed 11-year sentences after admitting the same offence. Sahan admitted to carrying out a crime of "sexual assault without penetration” for which he was given a four-year sentence.

All eight men, including the five self-confessed sexual offenders plus Auque and Wade, who admitted to filming their victims being targeted, were handed two-year, three-month prison sentences. Sahan, Auque and Wade had already been granted bail ahead of the scheduled start of the trial and remain on bail. The convicted men also have to compensate the survivor with more than €100,000 (£86,000).

At least two members of the group are unlikely to be returned to prison because of time served on remand before being granted bail around nine months ago. Five others remain in prison pending a further court hearing next month, at which their defence lawyers are set to ask a judge to agree to their release from Spain once they have served part of their jail time.

If you've been the victim of sexual assault, you can access help and resources via www.rapecrisis.org.uk or calling the national telephone helpline on 0808 802 9999

News.title: 'Trolls say I’m breaking my vows but my injured husband wants me to find love'
News.content:
A woman who was trolled after finding love while her husband is in a care home has shared the incredible message he gave her to find a new partner.

Amanda Robinson’s life was shattered in 2021 when her husband Matt’s head was crushed in a sickening lorry crash. While he survived, his brain injury has left him struggling to remember his own children or hold a proper conversation.

Now, Amanda has finally found happiness with new partner Josh, who even helps care for Matt. But after being accused online of breaking her vows, Amanda has revealed the emotional moment Matt told her: "I just want you to be treated with true love."

Image: Amanda Robinson / SWNS) Amanda Robinson / SWNS) Image: Amanda Robinson / SWNS) Amanda Robinson / SWNS)

The mother-of-two learnt of Matt's collision in February 2021 when she received a notification from Life360, an application the pair had been using to monitor each other's whereabouts, which can identify if the other person has been involved in a motor accident.

Medical staff initially feared the now 42 year old wouldn't survive, and relatives were even instructed to bid their last farewells. Amanda recalled, "Matt and I had a great relationship. I was with him for more than half my life, I fully understand him. "We were so happy. "We laughed a lot. When I went to the hospital to see him, I was just in shock.

"I couldn't believe it was him, his eyes were black, he had blood coming out of his ears, eyes and nose, his head was all wrapped and bloody. There was literally no sign of life in him/

Matt defied all expectations to survive, and in December 2021, he eventually came home following a 10-month hospital admission. Yet this new phase proved anything but straightforward.

Amanda, 39, took on the role of Matt's full-time carer, helping him with tasks ranging from personal hygiene and getting dressed to eating meals.

Whilst Matt appeared physically well and retained his ability to walk, speak and consume food, the region of his brain governing personality, memory and behaviour had sustained damage, rendering him incapable of retaining information. Subsequently, approximately four months following the collision, Matt's eyesight failed.

Image: Amanda Robinson / SWNS) Amanda Robinson / SWNS)

In a video on TikTok, Amanda can be seen asking her husband what he would want for her in life following the accident.

He says: "I can't recall anything until it happens," before adding: "If something happens that creates a different relationship for both of us together, then it just will change to whatever way is necessary for you to have a certain meaning for the relationship you have with somebody.

"And it does need to be a certain way day to day for you, I just want you to be treated with the true love that you should be treated with. I say that out of honesty."

Professional nurse Amanda, from Prescott, Arizona, said: "Instead of seeing black, he hallucinates that he's in a places that he's not, it can get so confusing for him. In his mind, he's still living life before the accident. Sometimes he tells me he's off to work, other times he thinks he still goes to school. His memory is all over the place.

"The hardest part is having to explain to him where he is and what's going on. We went to our son's high school graduation, and I have great pictures of him smiling, but he has no idea that our son graduated high school.

Since the accident, father Matt can tragically no longer participate in conversation. According to Amanda: "If I say 'hi Matt', he will say 'hi' back, but he can't engage in conversation. "He will just sit there on the couch all day, he doesn't know how to ask for something to eat or drink. I have to tell him, 'it's time to get up now, it's time to get dressed."

Image: Amanda Robinson / SWNS) Amanda Robinson / SWNS)

Matt and Amanda first crossed paths whilst she was still attending secondary school, back in 2003.

They tied the knot two years afterwards, in 2005, and subsequently had two children - now aged 20 and 16. She believes he would want her to be "happy".

In 2024, Amanda took the heart-wrenching decision to relocate Matt into a nearby care facility because she was finding it impossible to look after him by herself. She explained: "I used to feel so sad that he lost his career, he lost knowing his kids, he lost friends, he lost everything. He's just kind of physically here.

"But then I realised that up until that moment, we had a great life, and I don't think he would regret anything. I lost all the same things he did. I lost my career, I lost a lot of time with my kids, I couldn't do things with friends anymore. I realised that me and my sons needed to be happy too."

In April 2025, Amanda caught the eye of Josh, a 38 year old country music performer from Phoenix, Arizona, whilst he was playing at a local bar in Prescott. They swapped numbers and, several weeks later, Amanda turned up at another of Josh's performances. On this occasion, she revealed her and Matt's story.

She recalled: "I don't think he fully understood what I meant, but then I saw him again a few weeks later, and he said he'd looked at my Facebook page, which I used to update friends and family about Matt's condition, and he said, 'Matt sounded like a great guy'.

This fresh romance proved to be a "pleasant surprise" for Amanda, who hadn't expected to find love once more. She explained, "I met Josh in April 2025, and it was such a pleasant surprise that he seemed genuinely interested in who Matt was before his accident.

"I hadn't let myself connect with anybody before, because in my mind, I was married and I was dedicated to being a caregiver, and I had just accepted that's the life I was going to have.

Image: Amanda Robinson / SWNS) Amanda Robinson / SWNS)

"Everybody's fully accepted Josh now, and is happy to see me happy again. They see the respect that he has for Matt and my kids. "People online say I'm breaking my marriage vows, but I don't have a marriage anymore.

"I know that if Matt was aware of what was going on, he wouldn't want me to be his caregiver forever, he'd want me to be happy. "

Several months into their relationship, Josh began joining Amanda on her routine visits to the facility where Matt now resides. Josh would perform on his guitar for Matt, aware of his passion for music. Josh now also helps Amanda with logistical challenges, from hospital visits to transporting Matt's wheelchair, whilst offering crucial emotional backing.

As Amanda herself expressed: "I did this all by myself for years, and now I have somebody else right there with me, and I'm not doing it all alone.

Initially, Amanda's children found it difficult to accept that she'd met someone new, as they'd needed more time to come to terms with their father's unchanging condition. Nevertheless, her sons have gradually embraced Josh, with the quartet now sharing bowling outings and restaurant meals together.

Offering his perspective, Josh explained, "After hearing about who Matt was prior to his accident, I had nothing but utmost respect for the man he still would be today if he could be. I told Amanda that I wish he could miraculously recover so she can have her family back together and that ever that was the case, I would step aside and be so happy for her and her kids. I enjoy visiting him, and I care about him. Amanda is a wonderful woman, and I am sharing my life with her, Matt and their two kids. I wouldn't want any of it to change."

News.title: Kate Middleton stuns shoppers on surprise market visit with William to Tik Tok sensations
News.content:
The Prince and Princess of Wales stunned shoppers in one of London's busiest markets with a surprise visit. Prince William and Kate dropped by Borough Market today as part of an engagement to learn about people and organisations from across the capital.

The bustling food market is one of the oldest and most renowned in London and is known for offering high‑quality, sustainably sourced British and international produce. Among the stalls the royal couple visited was Humble Crumble, a London dessert business that grew from a single market stall into multiple locations and millions of followers of Tik Tok. It began in 2018 when founder Kim Innes started selling fruit crumbles from a farmers’ market stall in London.

The concept was centred on a single dessert style; crumble paired with custard and toppings, and quickly built a following through word of mouth. And William and Kate donned aprons and served up the treat to customers. By October 2018, the business expanded into multiple market locations, including Borough Market, Old Spitalfields and Camden, and later Covent Garden.

They also stopped by Trethowan Brothers, an award winning family cheesemakers, which produce small‑batch traditional cheeses whose stall has been part of the market since 1998.

Over the past 25 years, brothers Todd and Maugan Trethowan have been producing small-batch, traditional cheeses at their dairy in North Somerset with a focus is on artisanal quality. Their Borough Market stall opened in 1998, bringing cheeses such as Pitchfork Cheddar and Gorwydd Caerphilly to London, contributing to the market’s reputation as a destination for food enthusiasts.

Gorwydd Caerphilly, a raw milk cheese from Trethowan’s Dairy, has received multiple prestigious awards, including Super Gold at the 2022–23 World Cheese Awards, Best British Cheese in 2021, and regular gold medals at the British Cheese Awards.

The couple also visited Change Please, an award-winning social enterprise fighting homelessness through the power of social enterprise and great tasting coffee with all of their profits helping people experiencing homelessness in the UK and Ireland.

Change Please train people experiencing homelessness to become baristas and support them with everything they need to turn their lives around- a living wage job, accommodation advice, therapy, bank account and onward employment opportunities.

The Prince’s Homewards programme last year funded Change Please to run an employability programme in Northern Ireland, supporting young people through training and paid-placements through Homewards’ New Employment Opportunities Network.

While there, Kate showed off her barista skills making a coffee for her husband – but the brew was turned away for not being decaf.

Image: Andrew Parsons / Kensington Palace) Andrew Parsons / Kensington Palace)

Kate was guided through the art of creating a cappuccino as she got behind the counter and rolled up her sleeves. She handed the finished drink to William saying “I’ve made a coffee for you, William” but when he asked “is it decaf?” and she replied “no” he suggested it should be sold.

Kate carried the coffee with her when they carried on with their tour of the popular attraction that has been a focus for food lovers for decades. Staff member Lorent Maliqi had guided Kate through the cappuccino making process and said afterwards: “She did really well. I was surprised, she got the texture of the milk right on point – it was a great effort.”

t comes as William and Kate will be carrying out engagements in and around the River Thames today. Kensington Palace said: "Today’s engagements will bring together people and organisations from across the capital whose work continues to support the life of the river and the communities around it."

News.title: Horror toll of Iran school attack laid bare as victims pictured for first time
News.content:
Heartbreaking new photos have laid bare the horror toll of the missile strike on an Iranian school as the US and Israel launched their joint assault on the country.

A preliminary military report has found the US was "likely" responsible for the February 28 strike on the Shajarah Tayyebeh elementary school building in Minab, Hormozgan province in southern Iran, which killed at least 175 people, mostly children, after outdated intelligence identified the building as belonging to Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guards Navy. But Donald Trump has consistently muddied the waters around what happened, with the military Commander-in-Chief saying "I don't know" when he was asked about the report.

He and his administration will be forced to reckon with the consequences if the conclusion holds true as the investigation ends, with photos capturing the innocent lives lost in what could prove to be one of the most devastating US military errors in recent memory.

Iran war triggers 'significant' drop in UK holiday bookings to top hotspots

'Iranian school full of girls destroyed - and Trump’s first instinct was to lie'

Trump has tried to sidestep any blame for civilian deaths in Iran, with the President saying last week that it was his "opinion" the Tomahawk missile evidence suggests hit the school was used by the defending nation. The US is the only known force engaged in the conflict alongside Israel believed to have access to the munition.

Speaking to reporters aboard Air Force One on Saturday, he said: "In my opinion, based on what I’ve seen, that was done by Iran. They’re very inaccurate, as you know, with their munitions. They have no accuracy whatsoever. It was done by Iran."

The Pentagon has followed its lead, with chief spokesperson Sean Parnell saying Tehran has become "increasingly desperate", and is "deliberately positioning missile and drone launchers in densely populated civilian neighborhoods, near distribution centres, and inside residential areas".

Thousands of people have died during the war so far, many of whom are civilians, and devastating images show civilians laying people to rest in mass graves following strikes.

News.title: Brit child 'filmed in Mallorca airport toilet' as man could face years in prison
News.content:
A man accused of secretly filming two women in an airport toilet could face more than six years behind bars.

The 41-year-old Spaniard has been charged after allegedly targeting two women, including a British tourist who was just 17 and therefore a minor, at Palma de Mallorca Airport.

Prosecutors said in court documents released last night: “The accused accessed the women’s toilet at Palma de Mallorca Airport and entered one of the cubicles where he bent down and managed to capture images of his victim, who was a minor at the time.

“He did so as she was in the cubicle next to him where she was changing her underwear.” The tourist has been summoned to give evidence at trial, a date has not yet been set.

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

A second woman was also allegedly filmed by the same man, understood to have been working for a private hire transfer company. He allegedly hid a mobile behind a waste bin in the same bathroom in the airport arrivals area, and filmed her going to the toilet. There is no evidence to suggest the suspect shared the images he recorded with anyone else.

Public prosecutors are seeking a two year six month prison sentence for a breach of privacy crime in relation to the offence against the older woman, allegedly committed on July 2 2023.

For the incident involving the British minor on July 7 2024, they say they want a four year jail sentence if convicted as charged. The man could also face a four-figure compensation demand believed to be near the £5,000 mark.

The full circumstances of his arrest were not immediately clear today. He was bailed after a night in a police cell pending an ongoing criminal probe which led to charges being laid.

No date for his trial has yet been set.

News.title: Putin rakes in $6billion in oil money from Iran war as tankers hit in deadly attacks
News.content:
Putin rakes in $6billion in oil money from Iran war as tankers hit in deadly attacks

The US-Israeli war on Iran has had an unexpected benefit for Vladimir Putin as he stacks the cash from oil sales amid conflict in the Middle East

View 3 Images Vladimir Putin has pocketed half a billion a day from oil sales, a new study finds (Image: POOL/AFP via Getty Images)

Evil despot Vladimir Putin has been raking in billions as oil prices surge following the US-Israeli war with Iran.

Despite years of sanctions on the Russian oil industry, amid Putin's deadly ongoing invasion of Ukraine, the outbreak of war in the Middle East has helped line the despot's pockets. New analysis has revealed the dictator is pocketing a staggering half a billion each day while oil supplies through the Middle East are disrupted.

The Strait of Hormuz - where one fifth of the world's oil supply passes - has been effectively closed since the start of the month as Tehran launches retaliatory strikes on neighbouring nations and ships in the passage.

View 3 Images An oil tanker on fire after it was attacked by Iran in Iraqi waters on Thursday (Image: Open Source Intel / X)

Vladimir Putin plotting 'ambitious' space missions to Venus and Moon READ MORE :

Oil and gas exports have been throttled with companies and countries struck with fear that their tankers could be the subject of Iranian drone attacks or bombings. This has led to some countries turning to evil Putin for their supply.

The Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air (CREA) has produced new analysis which shows Russia has pocketed £6billion in less than two weeks from fossil fuel exports. This massive amount is 14% higher than levels in February, according to The Sun.

Sanctions campaigner, at Urgewald, Alexander Kirk warned of the consequences war in the Middle East could have in what he called the "reality of fossil fuel geopolitics". He said: "When markets panic, authoritarian exporters cash in."

View 3 Images A Thai cargo ship after it was attacked near the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday (Image: ROYAL THAI NAVY/AFP via Getty Im)

The campaigner also warned how this massive influx of money could help Putin's deadly war, adding: "In less than two weeks, Russia has earned an estimated £6billion from fossil fuel exports, money that ultimately feeds its war machine. Easing sanctions now would not stabilise markets. What it would do is allow Russia to sell the same oil for a far better price.

"US sanctions have forced Russian crude to trade at a steep discount. Any rollback closes that gap overnight and hands the Kremlin a revenue boost worth billions, at the very moment that pressure is starting to bite."

Several vessels have been attacked in the waters around the Strait of Hormuz, including two oil tankers that were bombed in Iraqi waters. Shocking images showed how the vessels turned into a ball of fire that left at least one person dead on Thursday.

According to Iraq‘s director general of the General Company for Ports, Farhan al-Fartousi, said: "Two foreign tankers carrying Iraqi fuel oil were subjected to unidentified attacks inside territorial waters, causing them to catch fire."

Article continues below

The official also revealed the ships were hit by Iranian boats filled with explosives. He told CNN that it had rescued 38 crew members but at least one person has died.

Iran has warned the world it should prepare for oil to hit a staggering $200 a barrel, double the already high price. A spokesperson for Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said it will not let "a litre of oil" pass through the strait. They added: "You will not be able to artificially lower the price of oil. Expect oil at $200 per barrel."

News.title: 'I went to Bucharest to see what the UK Government's new dog rules really mean'
News.content:
'I went to Bucharest to see what the UK Government's new dog rules really mean'

The rules will stay in place until the risk is 'judged to have reduced sufficiently'

View 10 Images Caroline Abbott in Romania

When the UK government announced new restrictions on the import of rescue dogs from Romania last autumn, the news briefly flared and then disappeared. For a moment, it sat alongside wider debates about migration, biosecurity and animal welfare, before being brushed aside by the next breaking headline.

‌



But around 1,500 miles away, on the outskirts of Romania’s capital city, Bucharest, the issues behind that announcement never went away.

‌



At the Speranța Shelter — a rescue known as the “home of hope” for Romania’s dogs (Speranța means “hope” in Romanian) — around 500 animals continue to live, recover and age in safety. Many were pulled from kill shelters. Others were found injured, starving, abused or abandoned on the streets. Some will eventually be adopted by a Romanian family. Many will not. All are the responsibility of a small, determined team trying to solve a crisis that no regulations or adoption drives can fix overnight.

‌



View 10 Images The shelter is home to hundreds of dogs

I visited Speranța to see what happens after the headlines move on — and to understand how one pioneering shelter is quietly challenging assumptions about what “rescue” really means.

A rule change almost universally welcomed

Since October 7, 2025, and until the risk is “judged to have reduced sufficiently”, all commercial dog imports from Romania into Great Britain — including rescue dogs being rehomed — must be tested for Brucella canis before arrival. Test results obtained in Romania do not count; blood samples must be sent to the Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA) in Weybridge, Surrey. The dog must enter Great Britain no later than 30 days after the sample was collected.

‌



The Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) said the measure was introduced to protect both pets and people. Since 2020, there has been a steady increase in Brucella canis cases in Great Britain, with around half of infections linked to imported Romanian dogs.

Brucellosis is a serious disease caused by the Brucella canis bacterium, which can cause reproductive issues, joint pain and lameness in dogs, though many show no obvious symptoms. The risk to the general public is very low, but higher for those with frequent exposure, such as vets. If humans do become infected, symptoms may include fever and headaches — with young children, the elderly and immunocompromised people at greater risk of more serious illness.

View 10 Images Volunteers and staff at the shelter in Romania

‌



The new rules were welcomed by the veterinary sector. The British Veterinary Association called the decision a “vital step forward for animal and public health”, and some British vets now refuse to see dogs from abroad, even if they have been in this country for years, unless owners pay for a Brucella canis test or can prove their dog has already tested negative.

Many organisations that rehome Romanian dogs in the UK also support mandatory testing, saying it is already their standard practice. But others warned that the 30-day rule would be challenging to manage.

‌



Caroline’s Dog Rescue described the timeframe as “very tight.” Paws2Rescue said: “The timescales and delays will indirectly lead to more dogs in Romania on death row, not being rescued.” Just Kind Dog Rescue said the compulsory tests “add extra stress as funds need to be there for every dog” while Furry Paws Dog Rescue said it would redirect its efforts to countries that do not require the tests, such as France and Germany.

UK-based charities including Battersea Dogs and Cats Home, Dogs Trust and the RSPCA all fully support the regulations, arguing that they will help minimise the spread of disease and ease pressure on already stretched domestic rescue centres.

Battersea’s veterinary director, Shaun Opperman, said: “We’d encourage anyone thinking of rehoming a dog from overseas to be fully aware of the potential risks involved and to take great care from both a medical and behavioural perspective.”

‌



View 10 Images The rules have changed what people in the UK can do

The RSPCA said it’s too early to tell if the new rules will reduce the numbers of Romanian dogs being rehomed in the UK, but if this is the case, it could reduce the pressure on overstretched UK-based rescue centres. The animal welfare charity’s head of public affairs, David Bowles, said: “Importing a new pet from another country risks behaviour and disease problems – including the significant rise in Brucellosis cases we have seen in dogs arriving from Romania.

“It’s also vital that those looking to add a new pet to the family remember shelters across the country are packed with rescue dogs looking for a second chance of happiness. That’s why we encourage potential pet owners to rehome only UK-based dogs. Sadly, capacity issues in the rescue centre already mean we are in the midst of an animal welfare crisis, with animals coming into our care faster than they are being adopted. Importing additional dogs from outside the UK risks compounding the problem – potentially placing more dogs with the sector if rehoming fails, and meaning for every dog who is rehomed from overseas, one less UK-based rescue dog will be potentially adopted.”

‌



This point is often raised by people who don’t agree with dogs being rehomed from abroad, no matter how harsh and dangerous the conditions are for dogs, or whether the owner was turned down for adopting a dog from a UK-based rescue, which often has stricter requirements. More and more dogs are being abandoned as the cost of everything from pet food to vet bills continues to soar.

Dogs Trust said if the owner of a rescue dog was looking to give up their dog, the charity would first ask them to approach the organisation where they originally got their dog from. It said: “In cases where this isn’t possible, Dogs Trust would try to help, provided we have the resources and capacity to do so.”

Behind the debate lies a stark statistic: of the 67,552 rescue dogs legally brought to the UK in a single year, 32,525 — just under half — came from Romania. It is a country estimated to have between 500,000 and one million stray dogs — the highest stray dog population in Europe.

‌



View 10 Images The shelter cares for hundreds of dogs every day

Romania’s stray dog crisis — and a different response

Romania’s stray dog population surged during the 1980s, when the communist regime forced people to move from rural areas to cities and abandon their pets. Uncontrolled breeding, limited access to neutering and long-standing cultural attitudes towards dogs allowed the problem to grow.

One shelter taking a markedly different approach to the many which rehome dogs abroad is Speranța. Founded as the Speranța Foundation in 1996 by Florina Tomescu, who wanted to make a positive difference to the lives of animals, and expanded into a shelter in 2001 after 300 dogs were rescued from euthanasia, it is now led by her daughter, internationally renowned veterinary surgeon Anca Tomescu.

‌



Speranța is home to around 500 dogs including, at the time of writing, around 200 seniors, a dozen or so puppies aged two to three months, and eight paraplegic dogs who move around with the help of custom-built wheelchairs. It has 14 staff. At weekends, up to 20 volunteers visit to help out by cleaning bowls, playing with the dogs (if appropriate) and brushing them.

It is one of the largest private, fully regulated dog shelters in Romania. By contrast, Dogs Trust’s largest UK rehoming centre, in Evesham, has a maximum capacity of 140 dogs in its 80 kennel facilities on-site. Speranța is the first shelter in Romania to run a large-scale rescue while also focusing on improving the dog situation across the country through sterilising dogs, educating people and encouraging responsible ownership within Romania.

Speranța has never had a positive case of Brucella canis and has welcomed Defra’s testing regulation, saying it highlights the importance of robust health checks for imported dogs. But Speranța very rarely rehomes dogs abroad. Its philosophy is that exporting dogs does not solve Romania’s stray dog problem.

‌



View 10 Images Many of the dogs require seecialist attention

A quiet road to somewhere unexpected

As a lifelong dog lover, I expected visiting a shelter of this size to be a bit overwhelming. I imagined a sensory onslaught of barking and odours upon arrival, and an emotional toll that would require tissues at the ready. Instead, the reality was very different.

‌



Speranța’s fundraising and partnerships manager, Victor Gavril, collected me from my hotel in central Bucharest. After about 40 minutes of driving, we turned off the main road onto a long dirt track that appeared to lead nowhere. It ended at the shelter’s gates.

A couple of loose dogs wandered by the entrance. One barked briefly. Construction noise — a new food preparation area was being built — drowned out most other sounds. When we entered, a large, fluffy Romanian Mioritic Shepherd Dog bounded over to inspect us, stopping just out of reach.

Inside, we were greeted by Anca Tomescu. Since 2017, she has run the shelter her mother founded, and during my guided tour she demonstrated that she knew the name and backstory of every single dog.

‌



The shelter covers around 5,000 square metres and is long and narrow, with buildings or covered kennels lining both sides. Every concrete-floored kennel housed two to four dogs, with a straw-filled wooden doghouse for each one. Only a small number of aggressive dogs were housed alone.

Gates divided the kennels into a number of segments, each containing dogs with similar characteristics. In one section, large, energetic dogs fondly nicknamed “the horses” zoomed happily around the shared space while a caregiver cleaned kennels with a hosepipe and broom.

View 10 Images The dogs are cared for at a shelter in Bucharest

‌



Lives rebuilt, slowly and deliberately

One of the most striking areas was the section for paraplegic dogs. Most had lost the use of their back legs after being hit by cars. For several hours a day, they roamed freely in custom-built wheelchairs before returning to kennels equipped with heat lamps.

Meeting them was emotional — but not in the way I expected. Five queued patiently to be petted, calm and content. I realised they were better off here than in private homes. They had the company of each other and the staff, were free from nappies, with easy access to specialised diets, physiotherapy and wheelchair repairs.

It was incredible to hear that over time, five of the shelter’s 25 paraplegic dogs have regained the ability to walk after physiotherapy. It just showed that you should never write a dog off.

‌



Elsewhere, I met a cheerful three-legged dog whose front leg had been amputated, and learned that Speranța never puts a dog to sleep unless it is suffering and nothing can be done. Around 200 residents are over the age of 10, including a 21-year-old. Families sometimes choose to adopt senior dogs — even a 19-year-old found a home. “I couldn’t believe it,” Anca said. “It was wonderful.”

A friendly dog called Bach flopped onto his back at my feet, demanding a belly rub. I later remotely adopted him, maintaining a connection with the shelter long after I left.

View 10 Images

‌



More than a shelter

Speranța is far more than a refuge. On site are facilities for diagnostic testing, surgery, physiotherapy and behavioural rehabilitation. Being able to get blood test results quickly can mean the difference between life and death.

My favourite area was the open-air “kindergarten”, where more than a dozen puppies socialised and played until they grew cold and were taken back inside. Mixed breeds of every colour, they surrounded me with curiosity, tugging at my jumper with tiny teeth, before piling into bed to nap together. They would have no trouble finding a home.

Towards the far end of the shelter, there were plans for a new playpen, swimming pool and a quiet space where adopters can get to know dogs properly.

‌



Most of the dogs treated my presence with interest but calmness. Through the Messengers of Hope programme, trainers and behaviour specialists work on site and in local parks to help dogs recover from trauma and adapt to future homes.

Psychologist and educational project manager Iulia Miu explained: “They might come in very scared, or have been abused. We make them trust humans again.” She described Misu, a dog who once sat facing a wall in fear, and who now walks on a lead in public parks after months of careful work.

Education is central to Speranța’s mission. Teams visit schools, and schools visit the shelter, to challenge myths about stray dogs, discuss sterilisation and discover how to interact safely with animals. “We talk about a dog’s needs and stress signals,” said Iulia. “A lot of accidents come because children hug dogs.” Victor added: “We want to change people’s opinions of stray dogs. There are myths that they are all sick or aggressive.”

‌



Speranța regularly organises events to raise awareness and promote adoptions. Off site, these have included a campaign aimed at using the wide appeal of football to inspire people to give dogs homes. Players at first division matches walked onto the pitch holding rescue dogs in their arms. This idea has been replicated in countries including Spain and Russia. On site, the shelter holds a monthly event where locals, visitors, staff and even celebrities join forces to cook a meal for the shelter’s dogs.

View 10 Images Caroline with the team in Bucharest

‌



Why Speranța doesn’t export dogs

Romania has the third highest proportion of dog-owning households in Europe, with 45% of households owning at least one dog. Only Hungary (50%) and Poland (49%) rank higher. In the UK, 36% of households own a dog. However, Romania doesn’t have the same dog-loving culture that we do. Victor said that in rural areas, many people keep dogs outside on chains to warn of intruders, including bears. Also, many people let their unneutered dogs roam the streets. Unplanned litters are often abandoned.

Anca Tomescu, whose animal rescue work has taken her across the world including to India, Myanmar, Sudan and Lebanon, does not believe the stray dog problem will be solved in her lifetime — but she is adamant that kill shelters and exporting dogs are not the answer.

“No matter how many are euthanised or exported, the problem still exists. They reproduce faster than they are killed,” she said. “It’s easy to think you have saved dogs by sending them abroad but saving isn’t giving a dog to someone else, it means you assume the responsibility. I’m against transferring the problem from one country to another. You have to put the problem on the table and find a responsible solution, not sweep the problem under the carpet. It’s about connecting programmes like education and sterilisation, not just taking dogs off the street.”

‌



Anca, who has five dogs (all rescues, of course) and five cats of her own, said the attitude in Romania towards animals has changed in the last few years. “The new generation wants to do something and I salute them. My hope is they will change everything,” she said. “It’s a result of our huge work in our education programmes. If you have a dog, you will be more happy, more healthy and more responsible. You go out no matter what and do 10,000 steps a day. When you treat an animal with respect, you become a responsible adult and citizen. The hardest thing about my job is the people who harm animals for no reason. It’s your right not to love animals. But I can’t handle people who are mean to dogs for ‘fun’. When you abuse an animal, it’s a short step to abusing a human.”

Another of the ways in which Speranța has bucked the trend was by closing for adoptions during the Covid pandemic. “[If you had a dog] you were allowed to go outside more than a person without a dog,” Anca explained. “So there was a huge number of requests for a dog for only a few months. I said, ‘We can’t give you a dog for two to three months, it would hurt the dog.’ You adopt a dog for life, it’s not a toy. People called us monsters.”

The shelter is funded entirely by donations, with maintenance costs exceeding €30,000 (£26,420) per month. To date, it has carried out more than 100,000 sterilisations nationwide, found homes for over 1,000 dogs and inspired similar initiatives across Romania. In 2024, 128 dogs were adopted, which was a record. Last year, that figure was surpassed by July and a total of 242 dogs learned what the love of a family means.

‌



Speranța’s main connection to the UK is through remote adoption, allowing supporters to sponsor a dog’s care while it remains in familiar surroundings.

View 10 Images The dogs are well supported

After the headlines

Speranța’s adoptions and programmes are growing, and other shelters in Romania have asked for its help to do things better and become sustainable. The Speranța team is currently considering whether it could replicate what it does in other parts of the country, so although there are uncertainties about what lies ahead, one thing is clear – the future for stray dogs in Romania is in very good hands.

Article continues below

For more information about the Speranța Shelter, to view the dogs available for remote adoption or to make a donation, visit www.sperantashelter.org.

News.title: Questions mount for Hegseth over possible US involvement in strike on Iranian school
News.content: The letter highlights how the war has become an issue that divides US lawmakers along party lines. No member of President Donald Trump's Republican Party signed it. Meanwhile, the only Senate Democratic not to do so was John Fetterman, who has supported the military action, though said an investigation into the school strike was right.

News.title: Lloyds, Bank of Scotland and Halifax apps showed customers other users' transactions
News.content: It is unclear how many users have been affected - but one woman told BBC News she was able to see the accounts of six different users on the Bank of Scotland app, including some National Insurance numbers, over a 20-minute period.

News.title: Hundreds of GPs tell BBC they have never refused a fit note for mental health concerns
News.content: But many pointed out that it was especially hard to refuse time off for mental health complaints. One asked: "Who am I to say I don't believe that someone is struggling with their mental health? It's not like a chest infection where there is definite proof of illness."

News.title: Minister defends PM's handling of Mandelson appointment
News.content: He said Sir Keir has not sought to move on from the issue but instead is "understanding the damage that it has done and then putting in place changes to public life, to the very structures of power in our society … that have ignored what has happened to women and girls at the hands of powerful men".

News.title: My phone contents were shared with the police colleague I accused of rape
News.content:
Family's fight for answers after prison death 'felt like torture'

Allan Marshall was on remand at HMP Edinburgh in 2015 when he was restrained face down by up to 17 prison officers.

News.title: Draper fights to 'mind-blowing' win over Djokovic
News.content:
Britain's Jack Draper produced a superb fightback to beat Novak Djokovic and reach the Indian Wells fourth round - a result he believes could be "a real big moment" in his season.

Defending champion Draper regrouped impressively to beat 24-time Grand Slam champion Djokovic 4-6 6-4 7-6 (7-5) in a "real physical battle" of two hours and 35 minutes.

Djokovic, a five-time winner at Indian Wells, had beaten Draper in the only previous meeting between them at Wimbledon in 2021.

The victory is an important one for Draper as he rebuilds after a torrid eight-month spell plagued with an arm injury.

"To beat Novak is something that is mind-blowing," Draper, who is playing only his second ATP Tour event of the season, told BBC Sport.

"I've watched him since I was a kid, idolising him. Unbelievable respect for the player that he is.

"I still want to play better, and feel better on the court, but I think for how long I have had off the court to come here and play three tough matches already and play that one, it's huge for me.

"It's something I will probably look back on in a few months and think this is a real big moment for me."

World number 14 Draper is joined in the quarter-finals by fellow Briton Cameron Norrie, who will play world number one Carlos Alcaraz next.

News.title: Epstein used modelling agent to recruit girls, Brazilian women tell BBC
News.content: Describing the trip to France, she says: "He [Epstein] would give me $300 (£225). I would go out for a walk and give him the change, but he would tell me to keep the money. He would test me and leave money in my room, and then I would give it back to him, and he would say I could keep it."

News.title: Where did fridge-carrying fundraiser's donations go?
News.content: To the public, he was the fundraiser known for carrying a fridge on his back for hundreds of miles for charity. But, nearly five years after Thomas Judge started collecting money for mental health support, a group of former volunteers and staff say they have seen no evidence any of it has been used for charitable causes.

Boxing.title: Donald Trump gives Jake Paul shock endorsement for political career as boxer joins President at Kentucky rally
Boxing.content:
President Donald Trump brought boxing star Jake Paul onstage during a rally on Wednesday and made a bold prediction about the social media star's future.

The 29-year-old Ohio native, who has transitioned from a YouTube personality to a major force in the combat sports world, stood alongside the president as the crowd in Hebron, Ky roared in support.

During the event, Trump signaled that Paul's influence could eventually shift from the boxing ring to the halls of government in the coming years.

'I'm going to make a prediction that you will be, in the not too distant future, running for political office,' Trump told the fighter and the assembled supporters.

The president didn't stop at a mere prediction, immediately offering his political backing to the 'Problem Child' should he decide to pursue a seat in the future.

'And you have my complete and total endorsement,' the president added, cementing a public alliance with one of the most polarizing and popular figures in modern media.

President Donald Trump brought boxing star Jake Paul onstage during a rally on Wednesday

Trump suggested that Paul could shift from the boxing ring to the halls of government

Paul expressed his admiration for the president and credited him with teaching him the fundamental values of grit and resilience

Addressing the Kentucky crowd, Paul expressed his admiration for the president and credited him with teaching him the fundamental values of grit and resilience.

He told the crowd that the president had taught him the value of 'courage' and to 'never back down from a fight,' a mantra Paul has lived by throughout his boxing career.

'We need more factories like this thriving all over the country,' Paul said, emphasizing his support for the president's economic vision for the American Heartland.

'And I know [Trump's] going to be the one to bring that here to us,' he continued, signaling his belief in a resurgence of American manufacturing.

The fighter also leaned into a spiritual message, telling the audience: 'I know God is with us. I know he wants us on the right side of history, and everyone here has to do their part.'

'And God's got us. Trump's got us. God bless,' Paul concluded, before reflecting on the local spirit of the attendees in the Bluegrass State.

'I feel all the local Kentuckians feel the same way -- you guys have that fight, you guys have that swag. There's a lot of young kids in here. The future of America.'

Trump returned the praise, telling the crowd: 'He's a great guy. He's a courageous guy and a talented guy. He's a hell of a fighter, too, by the way.'

'We need more factories like this thriving all over the country,' Paul said, emphasizing his support for the president's economic vision

Paul's most recent endeavor in the boxing ring saw him take on British star Anthony Joshua

The endorsement of Paul's fighting spirit comes as the 29-year-old continues to dominate headlines following his endeavors in the boxing ring over recent years.

Paul has transformed the boxing landscape by leveraging his massive digital following to create events that have outdrawn traditional championship bouts in viewership and revenue.

His most recent endeavor in the ring saw him take on British heavyweight star Anthony Joshua, which ended in a knockout victory for 'AJ'.



Whether Paul follows Trump's advice and swaps the gloves for a suit remains to be seen.

Boxing.title: Anthony Joshua quits the UK to move to Dubai - even as thousands try to flee amid rising panic in Middle East warzone
Boxing.content:
Anthony Joshua quits the UK to move to Dubai - even as thousands try to flee amid rising panic in Middle East warzone

Boxing superstar Anthony Joshua is quitting the UK to move to Dubai - even though the desert city is now in a warzone with thousands of Britons desperate to get out.

The former heavyweight world champion, 36, has formally confirmed he will now be resident in the United Arab Emirates despite it currently coming under nightly attack from Iranian suicide drones.

Joshua, who was born in Watford of Nigerian heritage, won a gold medal at the 2012 London Olympics and has since been awarded both OBE and MBE honours - but now is quitting his home country.

The surprise move by 'AJ' - as he is known to fans - is revealed in the small print of paperwork he filed on Friday for his £150million business empire in which he is legally required to state the country in which he lives.

And, extraordinarily, the documentation shows that Joshua has pressed ahead with his decision to change his residential status despite the war between the USA and Israel and Iran which has seen neighbouring UAE come under sustained attack.

Anthony Joshua is moving to Dubai - as revealed in the small print of his paperwork

Footage taken from social media shows Saturday's drone strike on Dubai International Airport

As well as the weather and facilities, Dubai is popular with the wealthy because there is no income tax there.

Joshua would have been liable to pay UK tax on his dividend payment of £10.1million in 2024 and £6.3million in 2023.

His company Sparta Promotions also made profits of £20.396million and paid £6.65million in UK tax in 2024.

Joshua confirmed the decision in the same week that recent UK sport emigre Rio Ferdinand and his wife Kate spoke about what it is like to live in Dubai while it is under missile attack.

Those attacks have seen thousands of Britons living in or visiting Dubai when the war broke out left desperate to get out - with some paying as much as £100,000 to secure evacuation on a private jet.

Filings for two of Joshua's companies, Sparta Promotions Limited and 258 Investments Limited, both show the formal change in where he will from now on reside.

Joshua has long been a regular visitor to Dubai where he uses the renowned sporting facilities for holidays and training, as well as promotional work for brands he represents.

The most spectacular stunt in this vein came in 2017 when he undertook a training sparring session on the helipad of the famous sail-shaped Burj Al Arab hotel - a 90-foot wide landing platform suspended 700 feet above the sea.

Passengers in the terminal at Dubai Airport on Saturday after the airport closed again due to drone strikes

He visited the city late last year, dining out with boxing promoter Eddie Hearn, and again last month when he had training sessions with MMA fighter Khabib Nurmagomedov.

The striking decision to shift base to Dubai follows the tragic road accident in Nigeria last year in which claimed the lives of two close friends, personal trainer Kevin 'Latz' Ayodele and long-time therapist Sina Ghami.

Joshua has been in recovery ever since the ordeal and recently posted pictures of rehab work on his ribs via Snapchat.

He captioned the post: 'Rehabilitation protocol. When you go thru certain things, you realise that you are stronger than you think you are.'

The move makes Joshua the latest high-profile wealthy Briton to be based overseas following the likes of Manchester United owner Sir Jim Ratcliffe and EasyJet founder Stelios Haji-Ioannou, who both live in Monaco, and Pimlico Plumbers tycoon Charlie Mullins, who is in Marbella.

The rules on tax for those with ex-pat status are complex.

If individuals spend 183 days or more in the UK they are considered a UK tax resident, but if it is fewer than 16 days they would not be considered resident and eligible for taxes.

For in-between cases, HMRC officials have a complex 'Statutory Residence Test' to determine whether UK tax has to be paid, weighing factors like how much time is spent in each place and how established their ties are in both countries.

Joshua is pictured emerging from the car crash that claimed his friends' lives

Joshua pictured with friends Latif Ayodele (centre) and Sina Ghami, who both died in the crash

So to maximise any tax saving Joshua could be forced to spend barely two weeks a year here.

In December it was revealed that 'AJ' had spent £1.2 million to secure a 15,300 sq ft penthouse, the Sky Palace, in the new Park Lane 101 development in Sultan Haitham City, in Oman, which is 300 miles away.

But it is thought the apartment is an investment property and it is not clear if he has yet settled on a permanent base in Dubai.



There has been speculation that the attacks on Dubai could see property prices there fall by as much as 20%.

Boxing.title: Amir Khan heads to Africa again as boxing promoters battle for control of the sport
Boxing.content:
Amir Khan heads to Africa again as boxing promoters battle for control of the sport

Promoter Amir Khan is staging another boxing card in Africa, positioning himself in a rapidly shifting promotional landscape where global power brokers are increasingly battling for control of the sport.

Khan's latest event, set for Lagos, Nigeria, blends professional bouts with influencer boxing and regional rivalries - highlighting their strategy aimed at tapping into Africa's large youth audience and social media culture.

The card is topped by a crossover bout between Nigerian online personalities Carter Efe and Portable, who together boast around 10million followers and will settle a long-running online feud in the ring. It is being billed as one of the first major influencer boxing contests staged on the continent.

The event arrives at a time when boxing's promotional structure is undergoing significant upheaval. Frank Warren has been involved in a legal dispute connected to the Saudis, while rival promoter Eddie Hearn has also been navigating the growing influence of Dana White's new venture with TKO, Zuffa Boxing.

Against that backdrop, Khan has increasingly looked toward emerging markets rather than competing directly for the biggest heavyweight spectacles in the Middle East or Las Vegas.

His shows in Africa have mixed international fighters with local prospects and internet personalities, aiming to build audiences in regions where professional boxing infrastructure has historically been limited.

Amir Khan's vision for African boxing is moving faster than most realise. Behind the scenes, AK Promotions is already planning to stage four to five shows per year across the continent

October 2025 saw DAZN-broadcast 'Chaos in the Ring' card in Lagos, co-promoted with Dr. Ezekiel Adamu's Balmoral Group Promotions

The likes of Moses Itauma, David Adeleye and Lawrence Okolie all watched the event live

On the sporting side, the Lagos card features several all-African match-ups. Nigerian cruiserweight puncher Ezra Arenyeka (15-2, 12 KOs), known as the 'Nigerian King,' faces compatriot Godday Appah (14-2, 13 KOs) in a bout between two heavy-handed fighters from rival neighbourhoods in southern Nigeria. Both bring knockout ratios above 80 per cent, adding local rivalry to what organisers expect to be one of the night's most intense fights.

Ghana's Elvis Ahorgah (15-4, 13 KOs), who previously fought British contenders Callum Simpson and Tyler Denny in the UK, returns to Africa to face Newcastle's Joe Laws (15-4, 5 KOs) in a super-middleweight contest.

Elsewhere on the card, British southpaw Michael McKinson (27-2, 4 KOs) takes on unbeaten Algerian prospect Mohammad Sahnoun (8-0, 6 KOs), while teenage Nigerian prodigy Raheem Animashaun (19-0, 12 KOs) meets Tanzania's experienced Emmanuel Amos (22-10-1, 13 KOs) in a youth-versus-veteran clash.

Additional bouts include Nigerian prospect Basit Adebayo against Tanzanian veteran Loren Japhet, national middleweight champion Rasheed Adeyemo facing Nicolaus Michael Mdoe, and UK-based Ghanaian welterweight Samuel Antwi meeting Congolese fighter Paul Kamanga.



For Khan, the card reflects an attempt to carve out a niche as boxing's promotional map shifts. While Saudi Arabia has drawn many of the sport's biggest names and purses, emerging markets such as Africa offer promoters a different growth opportunity.

Boxing.title: Inside the shocking twist that has rocked combat sports: Eddie Hearn and Tom Aspinall tell CHARLOTTE DALY why they have joined forces just days after Conor Benn's stunning defection to Zuffa Boxing
Boxing.content:
Eddie Hearn insists this one isn't about revenge.

But sitting across from the Matchroom boss and the reigning UFC heavyweight champion Tom Aspinall, it is impossible to ignore the wider context.

Matchroom has spent more than four decades building sporting empires, from boxing to darts and snooker, yet the promoter is now embarking on something new.

Matchroom Talent Agency, a fresh subsidiary within the wider Matchroom Sport umbrella, has been created to represent athletes beyond the confines of their sport.

Commercial deals, media opportunities, publishing, digital, broadcast, legal support, fight purse negotiations - the whole ecosystem that surrounds a modern superstar.

And Aspinall, the Salford-born heavyweight king of the UFC, is the first name on the roster - signing a two-year deal with Matchroom Talent Agency.

'It's a two-year deal, a representation agreement that covers every aspect of what Tom does,' Hearn tells Daily Mail Sport. 'On the commercial side, we'll be building his profile, bringing new brands to the table across PR, media, social and digital. But we'll also support the fight side of things.

'This isn't about us stepping in and saying, "Right Tom, we'll go talk to Dana about your career." Andy Aspinall has handled that throughout Tom's career and he'll continue to do so, with our guidance and support. We'll handle the contractual side, our lawyers will review the deals, look at what's next, which fights are on the table, what the purses look like, and make sure everything stacks up.

Tom Aspinall became the first athlete to sign for Matchroom's new talent agency on Thursday

Aspinall hinted at an announcement hours before it was released via an Instagram post

'If Andy is the one having those conversations and we all agree that's the right approach. I'm perfectly happy with that. I don't necessarily need to sit in a room with Dana and negotiate Tom's next fight. But if Tom wants me there, and everyone feels I'm the right person to do it, then of course I'll be there, dealing with Hunter or whoever it needs to be.

'What we're not doing is coming in and saying we're going to break contracts or start arguments with the UFC. Not at all. We're a professional company. We respect what they do and we respect the contract. We'll just have sensible conversations about the value of what is, in my opinion, one of the biggest stars in the sport and more importantly, the flagship division of combat sports: the heavyweight championship.'

Asked whether the new partnership should lead to Aspinall earning significantly more money going forward, Hearn leaves little doubt about his expectations.

'Substantially more. And we’ll show every MMA fighter how we can drive value. I always talk about value. I have looked at some of the contracts he has received to be main event, you'd make three times that for a British title fight.

'Maybe I’m in the wrong business. And by the way, congratulations to them - they’ve made an absolute fortune. But now I’m on the other side, and I represent our guys fiercely to make sure they get what they deserve. When you look at what Tom Aspinall brings to an event and the amount of money that event generates, I don’t think it’s fair. So I have to give him my honest advice from that perspective.'

The venture sits alongside Matchroom's established sporting pillars: Matchroom Boxing, the Professional Darts Corporation, the World Snooker Tour and Matchroom MultiSport.

Many view it as a power move from Hearn who has been back and forth in a verbal war with UFC and Zuffa boss Dana White

Collectively, those properties have delivered some of the most recognisable sporting moments of the past 40 years while turning niche competitions into global entertainment products.

Hearn believes the same thinking can now be applied to individual athletes. Especially Aspinall.

'We’ve been accused by Dana and those guys of lacking vision. I actually think this is a great example of vision. It’s a wonderful move. It’s great for our business, it’s great for Tom Aspinall and, by the way, it’s great for the UFC as well.

'What we’re doing is helping drive the value of one of their fighters. When Tom Aspinall fights again, he’ll be a bigger star than he’s ever been before, which ultimately benefits the UFC. But first we have to make sure he’s getting the respect he deserves from the UFC.

'But not only that. This is a 360-degree management business. We're not just talking about contracts. We're talking sponsorships, brand deals, PR, endorsements, lifestyle, logistics, long-term career strategy.'

It is an ambitious pitch, but one built on Matchroom's long-standing experience of elevating athletes and sports to new commercial heights.

Under the Hearns, darts transformed from smoky pub competitions into sold-out arena spectacles broadcast in primetime television slots. The Professional Darts Corporation distributed more than £19million in prize money in 2024 alone.

Snooker has undergone a similar transformation, expanding aggressively across Asia and the Middle East since Barry Hearn began reshaping the tour calendar into a sponsor-friendly global circuit.

Aspinall (above) has not fought since sustaining a serious eye injury against Ciryl Gane

'This isn't new to us,' Hearn says. 'We've been representing athletes for 40 years in different ways. We know how to build profiles, we know how to deliver commercial strategies and guide careers properly. This is just the next step.'

For Aspinall, it wasn't as straightforward.

'Eddie doesn't actually know this, so this is exclusive, but I went into that meeting in Monaco thinking, I'm not going to do it. Eddie was paying for as a PJ and everything. And I was like I actually feel bad.

'But, I just really liked what Eddie and Matchroom were all about. And I really felt it was right from me, felt right from my dad, felt right for the other team members, and we just all agreed that this is the right move.'

The 31-year-old has become one of the UFC’s biggest names thanks to a string of explosive finishes and his laid-back Mancunian charisma. Yet he has not always felt he has received the recognition he deserves and, after facing criticism following his recent eye injury, Aspinall decided it was time to expand his team.

Still, linking up with Hearn was not a straightforward decision given the long-running feud between the boxing promoter and UFC president Dana White.

'I didn’t want to be part of it, like a pawn in this fight between MMA and boxing, or between Dana and Eddie and all that stuff. I didn’t want to be dragged into that. But Eddie and Matchroom put an offer on the table that I really couldn’t refuse, so I took it.'

Asked whether the partnership with Matchroom could risk souring his relationship with White or affect his standing within the UFC, Aspinall is unequivocal.

Aspinall (pictured on the left) insists he's ready to get back in the cage and 'beat the living daylights' out of UFC rival Gane

'You can choose whatever manager, whatever representation, whatever commercial advisor you want. It's nothing to do with the UFC. This is my business. This is mine and my team's business, and we wanted to expand the team.'

Aspinall goes on to add: 'Fighting is obviously my main focus. That's always number one. But there's a lot that comes with being in this position - media, sponsorships, different opportunities outside the cage.'

Matchroom's global network, he says, offers a platform that could amplify those possibilities.

'You look at what they've done across different sports and how they've built stars,' Aspinall says. 'They understand how to grow a brand and take someone's profile to another level.'

For Hearn, landing the UFC heavyweight champion as the agency's first signing is a statement of intent.

'Tom's one of the biggest combat sports stars in the world,' he says. 'If you're launching something like this, you want someone with that kind of profile and personality to lead the way.'

Still, it is difficult to discuss the move without touching on the simmering tension that has recently developed between Hearn and UFC president Dana White.

The feud ignited earlier this year when Conor Benn, a fighter Hearn had guided from his professional debut and defended fiercely during the drug-test scandal that threatened to derail his career, signed with White's Zuffa venture.

The Matchroom promoter and superstar Joshua will be embarking on the Matchroom Talent Agency venture together

Hearn admits the manner of the split caught him off guard.

'I got the email from his lawyer,' he says. 'That was the first I heard of it. I asked for a call but never got one.'

The promoter had publicly backed Benn during the controversy that saw the collapse of his scheduled fight with Chris Eubank Jr in 2022 after two failed drug tests, a period that left the boxer sidelined and facing intense scrutiny.

'That's why it was disappointing,' Hearn says. 'Because we stood by him through everything.'

When asked whether he would work with Benn commercially with Matchroom's new Talent Agency, Hearn paused.

'I will always have the conversation, but right now I can’t see that happening. I never rule anything out though, because I’ve learned in the past that time can be a great healer.

'Take Jarrell Miller as an example. At the time I said I would never, ever work with him again. And I know him, I quite like him as a person, but how I felt then and for a few years afterwards was that it was completely finished.

'But as six or seven years pass, your feelings can change. I’ve learned you can never say never in this sport. I can tell you how I feel right now, and right now I don’t think it’s an option. Maybe one day there’s a conversation. Maybe you pick up the phone. You never know.'

White, never one to shy away from a verbal exchange, dismissed Hearn's frustration in typically blunt terms, labelling him a 'p****' for complaining about the situation.

Hearn shrugs when the comment is mentioned now.

'Dana says what he wants,' he says with a grin. 'In the space of a week he's called me a p**** and said he would fight me.'

But there is a certain symmetry in what has happened next. While White moved into boxing by signing Benn, Hearn has aligned himself with the UFC's heavyweight champion.

'Look, this isn't about getting one back,' Hearn insists. 'This is about building a serious talent agency. But Tom is an incredible athlete and someone we believe we can help grow massively.'

In truth, the commercial potential of athlete representation explains much of the motivation. Just take football - an avenue they are looking to break into. The global sports agency market is booming, driven by rising player salaries, escalating transfer fees and the explosion of digital sponsorship opportunities.

In football alone, agent fees surpassed £700million worldwide in 2023 while transfer spending climbed to around £5.67billion. Elite agencies typically take between five and ten per cent of athlete earnings and commercial deals - a lucrative slice of a rapidly expanding pie.

Hearn, though, says the ambition stretches beyond simple deal-making.

'Most athletes have different people doing different things,' he says. 'One agent doing contracts, another agency doing brand deals, a lawyer somewhere else. What we're offering is a single structure that handles everything.'

That structure, he believes, will become increasingly valuable as athletes evolve into global entertainment brands.

'The biggest sports stars today aren't just athletes,' Hearn says. 'They're media personalities, entrepreneurs, ambassadors. You need a team that understands how to manage all of that.'

For now, Aspinall's focus remains on defending the UFC heavyweight crown and cementing his place at the top of mixed martial arts.

But the broader picture matters too.

'I want to be the best fighter in the world,' he says. 'But I also want to build something that lasts beyond fighting.'

Hearn finishes with a smile. Matchroom Talent Agency has only just begun its journey - but with the UFC heavyweight champion leading the roster, the promoter clearly believes he has landed the perfect first signing.

Boxing.title: Tyson Fury confirms his lookalike son, 15, wants to box as teenager shows off his skills in the ring - but warns he will face same problem as Ricky Hatton's boxer son
Boxing.content:
Tyson Fury confirms his lookalike son, 15, wants to box as teenager shows off his skills in the ring - but warns he will face same problem as Ricky Hatton's boxer son

The Fury family bloodline will see the next generation turn their hand to boxing after Tyson revealed his lookalike son's desire to become a professional fighter.

Former heavyweight champion Tyson is a father-of-seven and says his eldest son Prince John James wants to follow his footsteps inside the ring.

Prince John James, 15, is currently in Thailand with his father ahead of the latter's latest bout out of retirement when he takes on Arslanbek Makhmudov on April 11.

And while his focus is on facing Makhmudov, Tyson has a word of warning for his son - citing the scrutiny around Campbell Hatton, son of the late Ricky.

Speaking to Ring Magazine, Fury said of Prince John James: 'I think that he will box, 100 per cent. He wants to be a boxer and that's why he has been in camp, training away.

'That's all he wants to do and this is slow and steady building. There are a lot of eyes and a lot of scrutiny on the kids of boxers, especially when the dad is a famous boxing star.

Tyson Fury (left) has revealed his son Prince John James wants to become a pro boxer like him

Fury has given a word of warning though after scrutiny surrounding Campbell Hatton (left)

'There is extra scrutiny and people are quick to say "he's useless, he's not as good as his dad, yada, yada, yada." 'Just look at Ricky Hatton's son Campbell. Ricky was an absolute legend so his son got so much stick for not being as good as him. It was unbelievable and he was only a young kid trying his best, trying to dedicate his life to something.

'So I want Prince to be completely ready for when he has his first fight because it's very hard being the son of a legend.'

As well as his son, Tyson's nephew John Boy Fury is also with him in Thailand and reflecting on taking both under his wing the 37-year-old is not going easy on the pair.

'I'm not just teaching them boxing,' he continued. 'I'm teaching these boys that this is a commitment forever.

'And the first job they have to do is get up, make the bed and clean the room up. That's job one, no questions. Only then can we start our physical training. I'm training them not only physically but mentally as well.

'We are training the mind, body and soul together and these two boys are like sponges, they take it all in. And these months that we are going to spend together every day is going to shape the rest of their lives.

'It will build their characters. They get to meet different people, have different experiences of different cultures, eat different food, everything. This will open their eyes and broaden their horizons.



'It's brilliant having them around, they're young, aspiring boxing at the beginning of their careers and I just wanted them to see how the master does it.'

Boxing.title: Eddie Hearn claims Conor Benn offered him money to keep quiet over his controversial decision to defect to bitter rival Dana White's Zuffa Boxing
Boxing.content:
